coredata.dc:title: Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots for methanol electro-oxidation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xingwei
Lv, Yuhong
Pan, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots are successfully prepared, and their catalytic performances for methanol electro-oxidation are investigated. Firstly, lignin-based carbon dots (Lg-CDs) are prepared by the acid etching of lignin carbonization products. Then, Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots (Pt/Lg-CDs) are synthesized by the reduction of chloroplatinic acid in carbon dots solution. Although Pt/Lg-CDs catalysts aggregate obviously due to the deposition of Pt catalysts on the surface of Lg-CDs, the activity and stability of an optimal Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalyst are still more outstanding than commercial Pt/C (40%Pt) and PtRu/C (30%Pt+15%Ru) catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation reaction (MOR) in acidic solutions. A large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Lg-CDs-800 facilitate the uniform and stable deposition of Pt catalysts, and make the surface of more Pt catalysts expose to reaction system, thereby improving catalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lignin
Platinum
Methanol electro-oxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots
Electro-catalytic performance of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C
The structure and morphology of Lg-CDs-800
The morphology and structure of Pt/Lg-CDs-800

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CV curves of Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.
CV curves (a) and CA curves (b) of Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.5 M CH3OH solution.

(a) CV curves of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, PtRu/C and Pt/C in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution; (b) CV curves of three catalysts in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution; (c–e) CV curves of three catalysts in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution during 300 cycles; (f) the decay of forward peak current density with the increase of cycling number.
FTIR spectra of lignin-based carbon (800 °C) and Lg-CDs-800.

(a) The UV absorption spectrum of Lg-CDs-800; (b) PL spectrum of Lg-CDs-800, the inset was images of Lg-CDs-800 solution under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp; (c) Raman spectrum of Lg-CDs-800; (d) X-ray diffraction pattern of Lg-CDs-800.
TEM and HRTEM of Lg-CDs-800 and the particle size distribution.
TEM and HRTEM images of Pt/Lg-CDs-800.
X-ray diffraction patterns of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C.
XPS core level spectra of C1s (a) and Pt4f (b) for Pt/Lg-CDs-800, C1s (c) and Pt4f (d) for Pt/C, C1s (e) and Pt4f (f) for PtRu/C.
Fitting results summary of C functional groups obtained from XPS.
Fitting results summary of Pt 4f obtained from XPS.

head.ce:title: Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots for methanol electro-oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials
Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots are successfully prepared, and their catalytic performances for methanol electro-oxidation are investigated. Firstly, lignin-based carbon dots (Lg-CDs) are prepared by the acid etching of lignin carbonization products. Then, Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots (Pt/Lg-CDs) are synthesized by the reduction of chloroplatinic acid in carbon dots solution. Although Pt/Lg-CDs catalysts aggregate obviously due to the deposition of Pt catalysts on the surface of Lg-CDs, the activity and stability of an optimal Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalyst are still more outstanding than commercial Pt/C (40%Pt) and PtRu/C (30%Pt+15%Ru) catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation reaction (MOR) in acidic solutions. A large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Lg-CDs-800 facilitate the uniform and stable deposition of Pt catalysts, and make the surface of more Pt catalysts expose to reaction system, thereby improving catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, with fast consumption of fossil fuels and environment pollution, the demand of clean energy increases dramatically [1]. Direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) is an ideal power source for vehicles, and has broad prospects because of high specific energy, high energy conversion efficiency and environmental protection [2,3]. However, the lifetime and cost of methanol anode catalysts have hindered the further development of DMFC. PtRu/C catalyst is an effective anode catalyst, and has been a commercial product, but its price is still high, and the catalytic activity and durability are also not satisfactory [4,5]. Therefore, the development of anode catalysts with excellent performance and low cost is an urgent problem.

Developing appropriate carbon supports for Pt catalysts is an effective way to improve the activity and durability of anode catalysts, which has been studying for a long time [2]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of nanocarbon materials, have been the focus of research ever since discovered in 2004 [6]. With sizes below 10 nm, CDs are generally quasi-spherical nanoparticles composed mainly of amorphous carbon. CDs can be easily functionalized and surface passivated due to plenty of oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces [7]. With excellent optical properties and biological properties, CDs have many applications such as optoelectronics [8], biomedicine [9] and chemical sensing [10]. Moreover, CDs have large specific surface area and good conductivity [11], and oxygen-containing groups on the surfaces can build a strong interaction between CDs and metal catalysts [12], therefore they also have potential to be ideal supports.

A few groups have already investigated the application of carbon dots for methanol electro-oxidation [13–15], but most of these works paid close attention to the composite of carbon dots and other carbon materials. For example, Tianzeng Hong et al [13] successfully synthesized Pt-carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide catalysts, and they suggested that carbon quantum dots could play multiple roles in the enhancement of Pt performance for methanol electro-oxidation. Jingjia Zhang et al [14] obtained the composite of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes via hydrothermal treatment, and they found that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots could improve the dispersion of carbon nanotubes and Pt catalysts, thus improving catalytic performance.

Recently, CDs obtained from various inexpensive biomass carbon sources have been widely reported, such as willow leaves [16], hair fibers [17] and raw cashew gum [18], which they possessed excellent and unique properties because of heteroatom and oxygen-containing groups in above carbon sources [19]. As the second most abundant renewable material just after cellulose, lignin (Lg) is a kind of complex and amorphous natural aromatic polymer with three-dimensional network structure, which is widely distributed in nature. It is mainly composed of benzene propane unit and is connected by ether bond or carbon-carbon bond [20–22]. However, the most of lignin is discarded directly and a few can be effectively utilized [23]. In recent years, many researchers have paid attention to the deeper utilization of lignin and done a lot of relevant researches. Such as, Xianlei Zhang et al [24] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon nanoplatelets from carbonizing alkaline lignin, and its catalytic performance for oxygen reduction reaction was investigated. Recently, Niu Na et al [25] obtained carbon dots from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin by ethanol extraction, and successfully applied them to biological imaging.

Here, the preparation of Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots and their catalytic performances for methanol electro-oxidation were firstly reported. In this work, lignin was carbonized at 500, 700 and 800 °C, and then lignin-based carbon dots (Lg-CDs-500, Lg-CDs-700, Lg-CDs-800) were prepared by etching carbonization products in a concentrated acid (30 mL H2SO4 and 10 mL HNO3). Pt catalysts supported on different lignin-based carbon dots (Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700, Pt/Lg-CDs-800) were synthesized by the reduction of chloroplatinic acid in corresponding carbon dots solution. Among them, the electro-catalytic performance of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 for methanol oxidation was comparable to that of commercial Pt/C (40 Pt) and PtRu/C (30%Pt+15%Ru).

Lignin was supplied by South China University of Technology, which was derived from concentrated acid pulping of black liquor. H2PtCl6 and NaBH4 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Nafion® solution was bought from Dupont. Commercial Pt/C (40%Pt) and PtRu/C (30% Pt + 15% Ru) catalysts were purchased from Johnson Matthey (JM) Company. All other reagents were analytical pure.

10 g of lignin was placed in a cone flask (500 mL). 200 mL of concentrated hydrochloric acid (mass fraction 37%) was added, and the solution was stirred for 3 h at room temperature. The product was filtered and washed with de-ionized water for several times. 2 g of purified lignin (put into the porcelain boat) was placed in a tube furnace (GSL-1500X) in an argon atmosphere, the temperature was increased to 500, 700 and 800 ℃ at the rate of 2 ℃ min−1 and preserved for 2 h, respectively. After dropping to the room temperature, carbonization products of lignin at different carbonization temperatures (500, 700 and 800 ℃) were obtained and ground.

0.6 g of carbonization products and concentrated acid (30 mL H2SO4 and 10 mL HNO3) were mixed, and reacted in a round bottom flask in 120 ℃ for 8 h. The reaction solution was neutralized with NaOH to pH = 7 in an ice bath, carbon dots solution was dialyzed in dialysis bags (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 5 days. Finally, a series of lignin-based carbon dots powders (Lg-CDs-500, Lg-CDs-700 and Lg-CDs-800) were obtained by freeze-drying method, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mg of Lg-CDs-500, Lg-CDs-700 and Lg-CDs-800 were dispersed in 10 mL of de-ionized water, 2 mL of H2PtCl6 solution (0.0386 M) and 80 mg NaBH4 were added sequentially, the volume of reaction system was controlled to be 15 mL. The above mixture was stirred at 55 ℃ for 3 h, and then the black suspension was centrifuged for 4–5 times to remove impurities. After drying for several hours at 70℃, Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalysts were obtained. The content of Pt was 62.1% for Pt/Lg-CDs-500, 61.5% for Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and 60.2% for Pt/Lg-CDs-800, which they were obtained based on XPS results. From the point of view of membrane electrode assembly, it is valuable to load a high content of Pt on a support for reducing the size and weight of fuel cells. However, the uniform dispersion of high load Pt catalysts was difficult for conventional carbon materials.

The ultraviolet visible absorption spectrum was conducted in 756S UV–vis spectrometer with a wavelength range of 200˜800 nm. The infrared spectrum was characterized with a NICOLET 5700 instrument. The fluorescence spectrum was measured in Fluorolog-3-P with a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The JEOL JEM-2100 instrument was used to characterize the morphology of carbon dots and catalysts. Moreover, wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of as-prepared catalysts were also characterized by D/max2550VB/PC (Rigaku, Co, Japan) and ESCALAB 250Xi.

The electrochemical tests were carried out using a CHI660D with a three-electrode system. The reference electrode was Hg/Hg2SO4, and the auxiliary electrode was a large area of glassy carbon electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 to calculate the electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of Pt catalysts. The electro-catalytic performance of methanol oxidation was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). CV test was conducted in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. CA measurement was tested at a constant potential of 0.15 V (vs Hg/Hg2SO4) for 1000s. In order to compare the stability of catalysts, CV curves of 100, 200 and 300 cycles were recorded in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. Electrolyte (0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4) was replaced per 100 cycles to eliminate the effect of methanol concentration attenuation. In contrast, commercial Pt/C and PtRu/C were also tested under the same condition.

The preparation process of the catalytic electrode was as follows. First, the glassy carbon electrode with a diameter of 3 mm was polished with the alumina powder of 0.05 μm and washed with de-ionized water for several times, and then dried in a nitrogen atmosphere. 2 mg of catalysts were dispersed in 1 mL de-ionized water. After 30 min of ultrasound, a homogeneous suspension was formed. 5 μL of above suspension was dropped into the glassy carbon electrode under nitrogen atmosphere. After drying, 5 μL of Nafion® solution (wt 5%) was dripped to cover the surface of catalysts. When the solvent evaporated, the catalytic electrode was obtained for the subsequent tests.

To optimize carbonization temperature, the performance of different Pt/Lg-CDs catalysts was compared. Fig. 1 depicted CV curves of Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 in 0.5 M H2SO4. The electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of Pt catalysts was calculated according to the integral area of hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks. The formula is as follows: [26]
(1)
E A S A =
Q H
0.21 ×
M
P t

"QH" is hydrogen adsorption charge which integrates from hydrogen region of CV curve, assuming that hydrogen atoms are monolayer covered in the smooth surface of Pt, the charge value is a constant of 0.21 MC cm−2. QH value is calculated from cyclic voltammograms after excluding the influence of the charge from the electric double layer. "MPt" is the loading of Pt on the working electrode. Based on Fig.1, EASA of Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 was 7.5, 26.7 and 40.6 m2 g-1 Pt, respectively. Due to a similar Pt loading, the above results indicated that Pt catalysts on the surface of Lg-CDs-800 had better dispersion than on the surface of Lg-CDs-700 and Lg-CDs-500, which made surfaces of more Pt catalysts expose to reaction system, thereby improving catalytic activity. A good dispersion also meant a high utilization of precious metals for supported catalysts.

Fig. 2 gave the electro-catalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts, which was conducted in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 0.5 M CH3OH. In Fig. 2a, the forward peak current density was 0.37 mA cm−2 Pt for Pt/Lg-CDs-500, 0.61 mA cm−2 Pt for Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and 0.76 mA cm−2 Pt for Pt/Lg-CDs-800. As shown in Fig. 2b, after polarizing 1000s at a fixed potential of 0.15 V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4), the peak current density of Pt/Lg-CDs-500, Pt/Lg-CDs-700 and Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalysts were 0.06, 0.25 and 0.26 mA cm-2 Pt. Obviously, the carbonization temperature had an important effect on the performance of as-prepared catalysts. Jun Cao et al [27] found that the amorphous carbon was basically formed in the temperature of 500–900 °C. When the temperature increased, the ratio of aromatic carbons/aliphatic carbons rose, and highly-condensed aromatic rings began to form. The high temperature was beneficial to the removal of impurities, but the high temperature reduced the yield of Lg-CDs and the energy consumption was high. Therefore, Pt/Lg-CDs-800 was chosen as the object of comparison with Pt/C and PtRu/C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electro-catalytic performance of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C
ce:section.ce:para: In many electro-catalytic studies, the current densities can be normalized by the electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of Pt catalysts. And the integral area of hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks are usually used to evaluate EASA of Pt catalysts. Fig. 3 a was CV curves of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C in 0.5 M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The characteristic hydrogen adsorption/desorption peaks of Pt (110) and (100) crystal faces could be clearly seen between −0.3 to −0.5 V (vs. Hg/Hg2SO4). Based on Fig. 3a, EASA of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, PtRu/C and Pt/C was 40.6, 33.8 and 31.9 m2 g−1Pt, respectively. A high EASA indicated that the Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalyst had a lot of available electrochemical active sites, which was important for improving catalytic activity [28].

In order to compare the electro-catalytic performance of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C, CV curves of above catalysts in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4 were recorded. Fig. 3b showed that two anodic peaks were observed during the methanol electro-oxidation, corresponding to two different states of Pt surface. It is widely accepted that Pt occurs oxidation in the forward scanning, followed by methanol oxidation that takes place on the oxidized surface of Pt catalysts. In the backward scanning, the reduction of Pt oxide begins first, and then methanol oxidation on almost oxide-free surface of Pt catalysts can occur at a low potential [29,30]. Therefore, the forward peak current density is the major parameter to evaluate the electro-catalytic performance. In Fig. 3b, the forward peak current density of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalyst was 0.76 mA cm−2 Pt, while that of PtRu/C and Pt/C catalysts was 0.65 and 0.37 mA cm−2 Pt. Fig. 3c–e gave CV curves of all catalysts during 300 cycles (9600 s) in 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4. And Fig. 3f showed the decay of peak current density with the increase of cycling number. In 300 cycles, the peak current density attenuation of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 catalyst was from 0.76 to 0.48 mA cm−2 Pt, with a retention rate of 63%. While that of PtRu/C was attenuated from 0.65 to 0.32 mA cm−2 Pt, which its retention rate was only 49%. Although the retention rate of Pt/C (65%) was slightly higher than that of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 (63%), the peak current density of Pt/C was 0.24 mA cm−2 Pt after 300 cycles, which was only 1/2 of Pt/Lg-CDs-800. On the whole, Pt/Lg-CDs-800 showed a better catalytic performance than PtRu/C and Pt/C for methanol electro-oxidation. This improvement should be attributed to Lg-CDs. Lg-CDs with a large specific surface area and abundant oxygen-containing groups increased the nucleation density of Pt atoms to avoid the aggregation of Pt catalysts in the reduction process, thus, an efficient utilization of Pt catalysts could be achieved. Moreover, a large number of oxygen-containing groups also played an important role for the stable dispersion of Pt catalysts due to an interaction between the support and the catalyst [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and morphology of Lg-CDs-800
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra (Fig. 4 ) showed the difference of functional groups between the lignin-based carbon (800℃) and Lg-CDs-800. In FTIR spectrum of lignin-based carbon, the stretching vibration absorption of ether bond appeared at 1073 cm−1 [33]. However, no obvious ether bond was observed in the spectrum of Lg-CDs-800, indicating that the ether bond was destroyed after the lignin-based carbons were etched by concentrated acid. Furthermore, Lg-CDs-800 contained a large number of oxygen-containing groups, which differed from lignin-based carbon (800 ℃). Among, the broad peak at 3403 cm−1 was due to stretching vibration of −OH, and the narrow peak at 1583 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of CO [12]. In addition, the peaks at 653 and 1429 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration of phenol hydroxyl and the stretching vibration of CC [34], and the weak absorption at 1328 cm−1 was owed to the stretching vibration of CN bond [12]. Above all groups also gave Lg-CDs-800 good water solubility, which was convenient for the uniform deposition of Pt catalysts on the surface of Lg-CDs-800.

Fig. 5 a was ultraviolet absorption spectrum of Lg-CDs-800 solution with a strong absorption peak at 228 nm, representing the π-π* transition of CC. The weak absorption peak at 270 nm was caused by the conjugated double bond of the benzene ring [35]. These absorption features indicated that Lg-CDs-800 possessed many chromophores, such as CC, CO, OCO [36]. The fluorescence spectra of Lg-CDs-800 were shown in Fig. 5b, the illustrations were digital photos of Lg-CDs-800 solution under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp. The solution was pale yellow under sunlight, while it showed a clear blue color under a 365 nm UV lamp. Similar to other literature [37,38], Lg-CDs-800 solution also showed the dependence of excitation wavelength. From 300 to 380 nm, the emission wavelength increased with the increase of excitation wavelength, and the strongest emission peak appeared at 380 nm. But the emission wavelength decreased with the increase of excitation wavelength from 400 to 500 nm [39]. The tunable emissions should be the result of different sizes of Lg-CDs-800 and different emissive sites on its surface [10]. From this, it could be sure that there were a lot of active sites on the surface of Lg-CDs-800 for the deposition of Pt catalysts.

Fig. 5c was Raman spectrum of Lg-CDs-800. A strong D band appeared at 1323 cm−1, which represented amorphous structures or defects in carbon atoms. The strong D peak was likely to originate from the large density of defects for the amorphous Lg-CDs-800 [40]. The weak G peak, corresponding to sp2 hybrid structure of C atoms, presented at 1538 cm−1. The intensity ratio of D to G peaks (ID/IG) was 1.4, indicating that Lg-CDs-800 should mainly be amorphous carbons. XRD result of Lg-CDs-800 powder further confirmed the amorphous carbon structures of Lg-CDs-800 (Fig. 5d). A wide peak appeared at 2θ = 24.5°, corresponding to the crystal face of C (002) [14].

Fig. 6 was the morphology of Lg-CDs-800 under high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Many particles could be observed in Fig. 6a, and the particle size distribution was calculated by statistical method. Fig. 6b indicated that the particle size ranged from 2 to7 nm, and the average size was about 4.7 nm. Lattice stripes of 0.26 and 0.33 nm could be seen in Fig. 6d, which were agreed with the crystal surface of C (020) and C (002) [41]. In addition, two staggered lattice stripes were observed in a single Lg-CDs-800 particle, as shown in Fig. 6d, indicating that Lg-CDs-800 was approximately quasi-spherical [42]. By simple calculation, the surface area of a Lg-CDs-800 particle, with particle size of 4.7 nm, was about 277.5 nm2. In theory, Lg-CDs-800 could provide adequate space for the deposition of Pt atoms with a radius of 1.83 Å.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of Pt/Lg-CDs-800
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 was TEM of Pt/Lg-CDs-800. Differing from Pt catalysts in the form of nanoparticles dispersed on conventional carbon materials (carbon black, porous carbon and carbon nanotubes) [43–45], Pt/Lg-CDs-800 appeared granular, and showed an obvious agglomeration. Based on the results of HRTEM, Pt catalysts should be heavily wrapped on the surface of Lg-CDs-800. In Fig. 7b, 0.227 and 0.196 nm of two lattice fringes were caused by Pt (111) and Pt (200) crystal planes [46], and C (002) crystal surface was corresponding crystal spacing of 0.332 nm [47]. These results further confirmed that Pt catalysts supported on Lg-CDs-800 had been obtained.

Fig. 8 compared X- ray diffraction results of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C. With regard to Pt/Lg-CDs-800, there were four sharp peaks at 2θ = 39.8°, 46.3°, 67.8° and 81.7°, which were attributed to the diffraction of Pt(111), Pt(200), Pt(220) and Pt(311) crystal face [48,49]. Because of obvious agglomeration, the average size of Pt catalysts supported on Lg-CDs-800 was calculated via the Debye-Scherrer formula:
(2)
d =
0.89
λ
K α
B
2 θ
c o s
θ
m a x
Where d is the mean dimension of crystalline particles, λKα is the wavelength of X-ray source, which is 0.154 nm. θmax is the angle of diffraction from (220) plane of Pt crystals, and B2θ is the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) for Pt (220) diffraction peak. Based on the above calculation, the average size of Pt catalysts for Pt/Lg-CDs-800 and Pt/C was 3.02 nm and 5.16 nm. The high dispersion of Pt catalysts on the surface of Lg-CDs-800 was further confirmed. Moreover, a wide peak was noticed at 2θ = 22.5°, it further proved that Pt catalysts had successfully supported on the surface of Lg-CDs-800. For commercial catalysts, the matrix material was carbon black, so there was also a wide peak appearing at 2θ = 24°. However, in PtRu/C catalyst, the peak at 2θ = 46.6° became weak due to the presence of Ru.

The valence states of Pt and the content of functional groups affected the activity and stability of catalysts. For this purpose, Pt/Lg-CDs-800 and commercial catalysts were analyzed by X- ray photoelectron spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 9 . It was not difficult to find the existence of CO and COH in C1s spectra of Pt/Lg-CDs-800, Pt/C and PtRu/C (Fig. 9a, c, e). The corresponding binding energies were 288.4 eV and 285.9 eV, but the ratios were different [50,51], as shown in Table 1 . Table 1 revealed that the content of COH for Pt/Lg-CDs-800 was about 23.4%, and COH could promote the transformation of methanol intermediates into carbon dioxide [52,53]. Moreover, Pt/Lg-CDs-800 had a high proportion of CO (65.1%). High proportion of CO provided more active sites for the deposition of Pt metal and strengthened an interaction between Pt and Lg-CDs-800, thus the stability of Pt catalysts was improved [54,55]. A considerable amount of -COH (32.9%) was also present in PtRu/C catalyst, which was one of the reasons for the activity of PtRu/C, but its CO ratio was 28.3%, which maybe lead to low stability [54]. In commercial Pt/C, there were only 8.3% of COH and 21.3% of CO, which further explained the effect of different oxygen groups on Pt catalysts. The peaks of Pt4f were calculated according to different valence area, as shown in Table 2 . The ratio of Pt°/Pt2+/Pt4+ was 37/31/32 for Pt/Lg-CDs-800, while the commercial catalysts were 28/38/34 for Pt/C and 27/21/52 for PtRu/C. Obviously, the content of Pt° for the commercial catalysts were both less than 30%, and a high content of Pt° indicated a high utilization of precious metals. Moreover, a high content of Pt° could also lead to a high catalytic activity, since Pt° was active for MOR [56,57]. Therefore, Pt/Lg-CDs-800 showed better activity and stability than commercial PtRu/C and Pt/C.

In this work, Pt catalysts supported on lignin-based carbon dots were successfully prepared. Electrochemistry results showed that the activity and stability of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 were more outstanding than commercial Pt/C (40%Pt) and PtRu/C (30%Pt+15%Ru) catalysts for methanol electro-oxidation reaction in acidic solutions. In 0.5 M CH3OH and 0.5 M H2SO4, the forward peak current density of Pt/Lg-CDs-800 reached 0.76 mA cm−2 Pt, and PtRu/C was 0.65 mA cm−2 Pt, while Pt/C was only 0.37 mA cm−2 Pt. The role of carbon dots mainly embodied in the following aspects: (1) Oxygen-containing groups provided good water solubility, so that Pt could be synthesized in homogeneous system. The presence of carbon dots reduced the aggregation of Pt catalysts in the reduction process to some extent and improved the utilization rate of Pt catalysts. (2) A variety of functional groups strengthened an interaction between carbon dots and Pt catalysts, thereby improving the stability of Pt catalysts. (3) The introduction of carbon dots increased the amount of COH, and COH was favorable for the conversion of intermediate products of methanol to CO2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51673064), and Shanghai Municipality Research Project (15520720500).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface profile control of FeNiPt/Pt core/shell nanowires for oxygen reduction reaction
